metadata.title: Open Source Supply Chain Security Landscape
metadata.description: Comprehensive mapping of open source supply chain security tools, frameworks, and standards across all major foundations and phases.
metadata.unifiedModel: Merges SLSA, S2C2F, SSDF, and CNCF SSC Best Practices into 8 lifecycle phases.

phases.name: Source Code & Version Control
phases.description: Securing source code repositories, enforcing access controls, branch protections, commit signing, and code review policies.
phases.slsaMapping: Threats A & B — unauthorized source change, compromised source repo

tools.name: gittuf
tools.description: Security layer for Git repositories using TUF-based delegation and trust policies for reference state verification.

tools.name: OpenSSF Scorecard
tools.description: Automated security health metrics for open source projects — checks branch protection, CI tests, dependency updates, signed releases, and more.

tools.name: CII Best Practices Badge
tools.description: Self-certification program for FLOSS projects meeting security, quality, and documentation best practices.

tools.name: OSPS Baseline
tools.description: Open Source Project Security Baseline — minimum security requirements for open source projects at different maturity levels.

tools.name: Allstar
tools.description: GitHub App that continuously enforces security best practices on GitHub organizations and repositories.

tools.name: Minder
tools.description: Policy-as-code platform for enforcing security profiles across repositories, artifacts, and development workflows.

tools.name: SARIF (Static Analysis Results Interchange Format)
tools.description: OASIS standard format for static analysis tool output interchange, enabling tool-agnostic code security findings.

tools.name: GitHub Secret Scanning
tools.description: Detects accidentally committed secrets, tokens, and credentials in repositories.

tools.name: TruffleHog
tools.description: Open source secret detection tool scanning Git history, filesystems, and S3 buckets for high-entropy strings and credentials.

gaps.title: Small project adoption barrier
gaps.description: Most tools assume GitHub/GitLab with sophisticated CI. Small projects on self-hosted git or alternative forges lack equivalent protections.

gaps.title: Insider threat detection
gaps.description: No open source tool effectively detects malicious maintainer behavior patterns (e.g., social engineering takeover like xz-utils).

gaps.title: Cross-forge policy portability
gaps.description: Allstar/Minder are GitHub-centric. No universal policy standard for GitLab, Gitea, Forgejo, Bitbucket.

phases.name: Dependency Management & Ingestion
phases.description: Evaluating, selecting, and securely ingesting open source dependencies. Managing transitive risk, license compliance, and trusted sources.
phases.slsaMapping: Threats D & H — compromised dependency, typosquatting

tools.name: Criticality Score
tools.description: Quantifies the criticality/importance of open source projects based on usage, contributor activity, and dependency metrics.

tools.name: Package Analysis
tools.description: Analyzes packages for malicious behavior by running them in sandboxed environments and monitoring system calls, network, and file activity.

tools.name: Package Feeds
tools.description: Monitors package registries (npm, PyPI, RubyGems, etc.) for newly published packages to feed into Package Analysis.

tools.name: S2C2F Specification
tools.description: Secure Supply Chain Consumption Framework — prescriptive guide with 8 practices and maturity levels for consuming open source securely.

tools.name: OpenChain (ISO/IEC 5230)
tools.description: International standard for open source license compliance programs — defines processes for managing OSS in supply chains.

tools.name: OWASP Dependency-Check
tools.description: Detects publicly disclosed vulnerabilities in project dependencies by checking against NVD and other vulnerability databases.

tools.name: OWASP SCVS (Software Component Verification Standard)
tools.description: Framework for identifying activities, controls, and best practices to verify the integrity and security of software components in the supply chain.

tools.name: OWASP dep-scan
tools.description: Next-generation security audit tool supporting SBOMs, vulnerability scanning, and reachability analysis for multiple ecosystems.

tools.name: Eclipse Dash License Tool
tools.description: Automated license compliance verification for Eclipse Foundation projects — checks dependencies against approved license lists.

tools.name: Renovate
tools.description: Automated dependency update tool supporting 70+ package managers with configurable automerge, grouping, and scheduling policies.

tools.name: Socket.dev
tools.description: Detects supply chain attacks by analyzing package behavior rather than known vulnerabilities — catches typosquatting, install scripts, and obfuscation.

gaps.title: Transitive dependency risk scoring
gaps.description: Tools evaluate direct dependencies well but lack depth on transitive chains. No standard for cascading risk scores through dependency trees.

gaps.title: Protestware / self-sabotage detection
gaps.description: No automated tooling reliably detects maintainer-initiated sabotage where code changes are intentional but malicious (e.g., colors.js, node-ipc).

gaps.title: Cross-ecosystem policy enforcement
gaps.description: Each package ecosystem (npm, PyPI, Maven, Go, Cargo) has different capabilities. No unified ingestion policy engine spans all.

phases.name: Build & CI/CD Integrity
phases.description: Ensuring build systems are tamper-resistant, builds are reproducible, and CI/CD pipelines cannot be compromised to inject malicious code.
phases.slsaMapping: Threats C & E — modified source between review and build, compromised build platform
phases.ssdfMapping: PW.6 — Configure Compilation, Interpreter, and Build Processes

tools.name: SLSA Framework
tools.description: Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts — framework defining Build L1-L3 with requirements for provenance, isolation, and hardening.

tools.name: SLSA GitHub Generator
tools.description: GitHub Actions workflows that generate SLSA Build L3 provenance for Go, container, and generic artifacts.

tools.name: FRSCA (Factory for Repeatable Secure Creation of Artifacts)
tools.description: Reference architecture for secure build pipelines using Tekton, in-toto, SPIFFE/SPIRE, and policy engines.

tools.name: in-toto
tools.description: Framework for securing the integrity of software supply chains by verifying that each step was performed as intended by authorized personnel.

tools.name: Tekton Chains
tools.description: Kubernetes-native supply chain security — automatically signs Tekton TaskRun results and generates in-toto provenance attestations.

tools.name: Witness
tools.description: Pluggable framework for supply chain attestation, building on in-toto — records evidence of each CI/CD step.

tools.name: Reproducible Builds
tools.description: Cross-ecosystem initiative ensuring deterministic build outputs — enables independent verification that source maps to binary.

tools.name: Buildpacks (Cloud Native)
tools.description: Transform application source into container images with standardized, auditable build processes — reduces Dockerfile attack surface.

gaps.title: Non-GitHub build provenance
gaps.description: SLSA provenance generators are mature for GitHub Actions but limited for GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI, and self-hosted runners.

gaps.title: Reproducible builds at scale
gaps.description: Despite years of effort, most ecosystems cannot achieve reproducible builds by default. Tooling exists but adoption is low.

gaps.title: CI/CD pipeline poisoning detection
gaps.description: No comprehensive open source tool detects pipeline poisoning execution attacks (PPE) in CI configurations.

phases.name: Artifact Signing & Provenance
phases.description: Cryptographically signing artifacts and generating verifiable provenance attestations to prove authenticity and integrity of software artifacts.
threats: Lack of provenance — cannot verify origin

tools.name: Sigstore
tools.description: Umbrella project for keyless signing infrastructure — consists of Cosign, Fulcio, and Rekor for signing, certificate issuance, and transparency logging.

tools.name: Cosign
tools.description: Container signing, verification, and storage in an OCI registry. Supports keyless signing via OIDC identity.

tools.name: Fulcio
tools.description: Free root CA for code signing certificates — issues short-lived certificates bound to OIDC identities, eliminating long-lived key management.

tools.name: Rekor
tools.description: Tamper-resistant transparency log for recording signed metadata — provides an immutable, auditable record of signing events.

tools.name: The Update Framework (TUF)
tools.description: Framework for securing software update systems through role-based signing, delegation, and key rotation — protects against various repository attacks.

tools.name: Repository Service for TUF (RSTUF)
tools.description: Production-ready service implementing TUF repository operations — enables package registries to adopt TUF for secure distribution.

tools.name: Notary / Notation
tools.description: Standards-based container image signing supporting pluggable trust policies and OCI-native signature storage.

tools.name: Model Signing
tools.description: Applies Sigstore-based signing to ML models — generates signing and provenance for AI/ML model artifacts.

tools.name: DPS (Device Provisioning Service) — OASIS
tools.description: OASIS standards for secure provisioning and attestation of device firmware/software — relevant for IoT supply chain signing.

gaps.title: Cross-ecosystem signing adoption
gaps.description: Sigstore is well-integrated for containers and Go/Python, but npm, Maven Central, Cargo, and RubyGems have varying levels of signing maturity.

gaps.title: AI/ML model provenance
gaps.description: Model Signing is experimental. No production-ready standard for verifying ML model provenance, training data integrity, or fine-tuning chain of custody.

gaps.title: Consumer-side verification tooling
gaps.description: Most verification requires manual integration. No turnkey solution for organizations to automatically verify all incoming artifact signatures and provenance.

phases.name: Package Distribution & Registry
phases.description: Securely distributing and hosting artifacts through registries, ensuring integrity during transit and storage, and protecting against registry-level attacks.
phases.slsaMapping: Threats F & G — upload modified package, compromise package registry
phases.ssdfMapping: PS.1 — Protect Software / Archive and Protect Each Release

tools.name: Harbor
tools.description: Enterprise-grade container registry with vulnerability scanning, content trust (Notary integration), replication, and RBAC.

tools.name: TUF (for registries)
tools.description: Provides secure update delivery for package registries — protects against replay, mix-and-match, and freeze attacks.

tools.name: Zarf
tools.description: Declarative air-gap package manager — bundles K8s manifests, container images, and Helm charts for disconnected deployment.

tools.name: OpenEoX
tools.description: Open standard for communicating end-of-life and end-of-support information for software products.

tools.name: OCI Distribution Spec
tools.description: Open Container Initiative specification for content-addressable artifact distribution — standard API for container registries.

tools.name: Dragonfly
tools.description: P2P-based image and file distribution system — accelerates large-scale container image distribution with integrity verification.

gaps.title: Universal registry tamper protection
gaps.description: No universal mechanism for tamper-proof registries across npm, PyPI, Maven, RubyGems. Each ecosystem has ad-hoc protections.

gaps.title: Namespace squatting prevention
gaps.description: No cross-ecosystem standard or tool for preventing namespace/package name squatting and typosquatting at registry level.

gaps.title: Non-container artifact registries
gaps.description: Harbor, OCI spec, and most tooling targets containers. Registries for other artifact types (npm, PyPI, Maven) have fewer open source security options.

phases.name: Vulnerability Scanning & Analysis
phases.description: Identifying known vulnerabilities, generating advisories, assessing exploitability, and prioritizing remediation through automated scanning and analysis.
phases.ssdfMapping: RV.1 — Identify and Confirm Vulnerabilities on an Ongoing Basis

tools.name: OSV (Open Source Vulnerabilities) Schema
tools.description: Distributed vulnerability database and interchange format for open source — aggregates advisories from multiple ecosystems with precise version-range matching.

tools.name: OSV-Scanner
tools.description: Vulnerability scanner using the OSV database — scans lockfiles, SBOMs, and container images with ecosystem-native version semantics.

tools.name: OpenVEX
tools.description: Open implementation of the VEX (Vulnerability Exploitability eXchange) specification — enables publishers to communicate vulnerability exploitability status.

tools.name: CSAF 2.0 (Common Security Advisory Framework)
tools.description: OASIS standard for machine-readable security advisories including VEX profiles — successor to CVRF, adopted by CISA.

tools.name: Fuzz Introspector
tools.description: Analyzes fuzz testing coverage and code reachability to improve fuzzer effectiveness — surfaces under-tested code paths.

tools.name: OSS-Fuzz
tools.description: Continuous fuzzing service for open source; has found 10,000+ bugs across 1,000+ projects. Free for open source projects.

tools.name: Trivy
tools.description: Comprehensive vulnerability scanner for containers, filesystems, Git repos, and Kubernetes — supports OS packages, language libraries, and IaC misconfigs.

tools.name: Grype
tools.description: Vulnerability scanner for container images and filesystems — matches packages against multiple vulnerability databases.

tools.name: SW360
tools.description: Software component catalogue and license management — tracks components, vulnerabilities, and license obligations across the organization.

tools.name: CoSAI (Coalition for Secure AI)
tools.description: OASIS collaboration developing frameworks for AI security assessment — includes supply chain security for AI/ML models and training data.

tools.name: OHDF (OASIS Heimdall Data Format)
tools.description: Standardized format for security assessment results — normalizes output from multiple security tools for unified analysis.

gaps.title: Reachability / exploitability analysis
gaps.description: Most scanners report all CVEs regardless of whether vulnerable code is actually reachable. Reachability analysis is nascent and per-ecosystem.

gaps.title: AI/ML model vulnerability scanning
gaps.description: No open source scanner can assess ML models for adversarial vulnerabilities, poisoned training data, or backdoors. CoSAI is pre-production.

gaps.title: VEX adoption and tooling
gaps.description: VEX/OpenVEX/CSAF standards exist but producer-side adoption is minimal. Most software vendors don't publish VEX documents.

phases.name: SBOM & Inventory Management
phases.description: Generating, distributing, consuming, and managing Software Bills of Materials for software transparency, compliance, and vulnerability management.
phases.ssdfMapping: PS.3 — Provide a Mechanism for Verifying Software Integrity / SBOM
threats: SBOM not kept in sync with releases
Inability to consume SBOMs at scale

tools.name: SPDX (ISO/IEC 5962)
tools.description: International standard for software bill of materials — defines format for communicating component information including licensing, security, and provenance.

tools.name: CycloneDX
tools.description: Lightweight SBOM standard for application security contexts — supports software, hardware, SaaSBOM, ML-BOM, and VEX in a single format.

tools.name: bomctl
tools.description: Format-agnostic SBOM tooling — merge, diff, filter, and transform SBOMs across SPDX and CycloneDX formats.

tools.name: protobom
tools.description: Protocol buffer-based SBOM interchange library — enables lossless translation between SPDX and CycloneDX formats.

tools.name: SBOMit
tools.description: SBOM attestation and integrity framework — links SBOMs to build provenance using in-toto attestations.

tools.name: GUAC (Graph for Understanding Artifact Composition)
tools.description: Knowledge graph aggregating SBOMs, SLSA provenance, OpenVEX, Scorecard, and vulnerability data into a queryable supply chain intelligence platform.

tools.name: OWASP Dependency-Track
tools.description: Continuous SBOM analysis platform — ingests SBOMs, tracks components across portfolio, identifies vulnerabilities, and manages policy compliance.

tools.name: Syft
tools.description: CLI tool for generating SBOMs from container images and filesystems — supports SPDX and CycloneDX output formats.

tools.name: Tern
tools.description: Container image inspector generating SBOMs by analyzing image layers and package managers.

tools.name: OSIM (Open Supplychain Information Modeling)
tools.description: OASIS initiative for unified supply chain information modeling — harmonizes SBOM, VEX, CSAF, and related standards.

tools.name: VSMI (Vulnerability Severity Metadata Index)
tools.description: OASIS work on standardizing vulnerability severity communication within SBOM/advisory ecosystems.

gaps.title: SBOM quality scoring
gaps.description: No widely adopted standard for SBOM completeness/quality scoring. Consumers cannot assess whether an SBOM is trustworthy or complete.

gaps.title: SPDX vs CycloneDX fragmentation
gaps.description: Two competing standards create tooling fragmentation. Translation tools (bomctl, protobom) help but are not yet mature.

gaps.title: SBOM consumption at enterprise scale
gaps.description: Generating SBOMs is increasingly automated; consuming, storing, querying, and acting on thousands of SBOMs is still challenging.

gaps.title: AI/ML BOM maturity
gaps.description: CycloneDX ML-BOM exists in draft but tooling for generating ML-BOMs (model cards, training data, hyperparameters) is virtually non-existent.

phases.name: Monitoring, Update & Incident Response
phases.description: Continuous monitoring of deployed software, automated patching/updates, incident response for supply chain attacks, and upstream fix contribution.
phases.ssdfMapping: RV.2/RV.3 — Assess, Prioritize, Remediate Vulnerabilities / Analyze Vulnerabilities
threats: No visibility into deployed component versions

tools.name: Alpha-Omega
tools.description: OpenSSF project improving security of critical open source projects — Alpha targets top-critical projects, Omega scales security analysis across ecosystems.

tools.name: OpenSSF Guides
tools.description: Collection of practical guides including coordinated vulnerability disclosure, npm security, evaluating OSS, and incident response.

tools.name: CACAO (Collaborative Automated Course of Action Operations)
tools.description: OASIS standard for security playbook automation — defines machine-readable workflows for incident response, threat mitigation, and remediation.

tools.name: OpenC2
tools.description: OASIS standard for command-and-control of cyber defense components — enables automated, interoperable response actions across security tools.

tools.name: STIX/TAXII
tools.description: Standards for cyber threat intelligence sharing — STIX defines threat information structure, TAXII defines transport protocol.

tools.name: OCA (Open Cybersecurity Alliance)
tools.description: OASIS initiative for interoperable security tooling — includes Kestrel threat hunting language and security tool integration standards.

tools.name: SCITT (Supply Chain Integrity, Transparency, and Trust)
tools.description: IETF architecture for transparent, auditable supply chain claims — provides append-only transparency registry for all supply chain metadata.

tools.name: Renovate
tools.description: Automated dependency update tool — creates merge requests for outdated dependencies across 70+ package managers.

tools.name: Dependabot
tools.description: GitHub-native automated dependency update and security alert service — creates PRs for vulnerable and outdated dependencies.

tools.name: Falco
tools.description: Cloud-native runtime security — detects unexpected behaviors, intrusions, and supply chain attacks at runtime using eBPF/kernel module.

tools.name: Kyverno
tools.description: Kubernetes-native policy engine — enforces image signature verification, SBOM requirements, and supply chain policies at admission time.

gaps.title: Multi-org coordinated response
gaps.description: No open standard or tool for coordinating supply chain incident response across multiple organizations simultaneously (e.g., log4shell-scale events).

gaps.title: Deployed component inventory
gaps.description: Runtime SBOM tracking (what's actually deployed vs. what was built) is largely unsolved. SCITT is promising but still in IETF draft.

gaps.title: End-to-end policy enforcement
gaps.description: No unified policy-as-code engine spans all 8 phases. Kyverno/OPA cover runtime; Minder covers source; nothing connects them.

gaps.title: Small project incident capacity
gaps.description: Most small OSS projects have zero incident response capacity. Alpha-Omega covers top projects but thousands of critical small projects are unprotected.

crossCuttingGaps.title: AI/ML Supply Chain Security
crossCuttingGaps.description: Almost no production tooling exists for model provenance, training data integrity, model poisoning detection, or ML-BOM generation. CoSAI and Model Signing are early-stage.

crossCuttingGaps.title: Consumer-Side Verification at Scale
crossCuttingGaps.description: Most tools focus on producers (sign, generate SBOM, create provenance). Consumer-side automated verification of all incoming artifacts at enterprise scale is underdeveloped.

crossCuttingGaps.title: Small Project Adoption
crossCuttingGaps.description: Most tools assume CI/CD sophistication, GitHub ecosystem, and dedicated security resources. Thousands of critical small projects cannot adopt these tools.

crossCuttingGaps.title: Cross-Ecosystem Consistency
crossCuttingGaps.description: Each package ecosystem (npm, PyPI, Maven, Go, Cargo, RubyGems) has different security capabilities. No unified standard spans all ecosystems.

crossCuttingGaps.title: End-to-End Policy Engine
crossCuttingGaps.description: No single policy-as-code system covers the entire supply chain. Tools cover individual phases but nothing connects them into a unified enforcement pipeline.

crossCuttingGaps.title: Standard Interoperability
crossCuttingGaps.description: SPDX vs CycloneDX fragmentation, CSAF vs OSV for advisories, and multiple VEX formats create integration overhead. Translation tools exist but are immature.

frameworks.name: SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts)
frameworks.description: Graduated framework with Build L1-L3 levels providing increasing assurances about build integrity and provenance.

frameworks.name: S2C2F (Secure Supply Chain Consumption Framework)
frameworks.description: 8-practice framework for securely consuming open source: Ingest, Scan, Inventory, Update, Audit, Enforce, Rebuild, Fix+Upstream.

frameworks.name: SSDF (Secure Software Development Framework, NIST SP 800-218)
frameworks.description: NIST framework defining secure software development practices aligned to US executive order requirements.

frameworks.name: CNCF Software Supply Chain Best Practices
frameworks.description: White paper defining supply chain security best practices for cloud-native ecosystems.

frameworks.name: OWASP SCVS (Software Component Verification Standard)
frameworks.description: Framework for verifying the integrity and security of software components across the supply chain.
